introduction: Inflammation of the dental pulp. The single most important disease process affecting the dental pulp. Accounts for virtually all pulpal disease of any clinical significance

features: Pain which is difficult to localize
May radiate to the adjacent jaw and occasionally to the face, ear or neck
May be triggered by cold or hot stimulants
May be triggered by a recumbent position
Occasionally triggered by mastication when food particles get into a carious cavity
The pulp can recover with removal of stimulus
Pain lasts for only a few moments after removal of the initiating stimulus
The pulp cannot recover even after removal of stimulus
Characterized by pain which lingers for at least one minute after removal of stimulus

goals: Exclude the pulp from the stimulus (or stimuli) in reversible pulpitis
Remove the pulp in irreversible pulpitis
non_drug: Conventional filling using amalgam, composite or GIC
drug: Paracetamol - Adult: 500 mg - 1 g orally every 4 - 6 hours (to a maximum of 4 g) for 5-7 days
Paracetamol - Child (every 4-6 hours): 13 and above: same as adult dosing
NSAIDs may be required in some patients
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Do not prescribe for patients with peptic ulcer disease
Aspirin is contraindicated in children less than 16 years as it may precipitate Reye's syndrome

prevention: Prevent dental caries (the most important cause of pulpitis)
